name: Iele
description: The Iele are mythical female creatures in Romanian folklore, often described as seductive and dangerous spirits of the forests, fields, and crossroads. They are believed to be the spirits of young women who died before marriage, or who were cursed. They appear as beautiful, ethereal beings, often dancing in circles in secluded areas, their laughter echoing through the night. Iele are known for their enchanting voices and captivating beauty, which they use to lure unsuspecting travelers to their doom. They are ambivalent figures, capable of both great kindness and terrible cruelty, depending on their mood and the respect shown to them. Iele are closely associated with liminal spaces and times, such as dusk and dawn, and their power is thought to be strongest during these periods. They are often depicted as possessing magical abilities, including the power to control the weather, heal the sick (though rarely), and bestow or remove blessings. Their presence is often indicated by strange lights or unusual sounds in the forest.
subtitle: Seductive and dangerous forest spirits
appearance: Beautiful, ethereal women, often dressed in white or wearing flowing garments. They may have long, flowing hair and captivating eyes. Sometimes, they are described as having cloven hooves or other subtle signs of their supernatural nature.
habitat: Forests, fields, crossroads, secluded glades, near bodies of water, liminal spaces
behavior: Iele are known for their seductive dances and enchanting songs, which they use to lure travelers. They can be capricious and vengeful if angered, but also capable of kindness to those who show them respect. They often test the morals of those they encounter.

keyMyths.title: The Lost Shepherd
keyMyths.description: A young shepherd, tending his flock near the forest, hears the enchanting song of the Iele. Drawn by their beauty and voices, he follows them into the woods, forgetting his sheep. He dances with them for what seems like a few hours, but when he finally returns to his village, decades have passed, and everyone he knew is long gone. He soon withers away, a victim of the Iele's enchantment.

keyMyths.title: The Healing Touch
keyMyths.description: A peasant woman, suffering from a mysterious illness, stumbles upon a group of Iele dancing in a clearing. Initially fearful, she is surprised when one of the Iele approaches her and offers a healing touch. The woman is cured, but warned never to reveal what she has seen, lest the Iele's favor turn to wrath.

keyMyths.title: The Crossroads Curse
keyMyths.description: A greedy merchant, impatient to reach his destination, disrespects a group of Iele dancing at a crossroads. Angered by his rudeness, the Iele curse him, causing his cart to break down and his goods to spoil. He is left penniless and alone, a victim of his own arrogance.

sources.author: Mircea Eliade
sources.work: Zalmoxis, the Vanishing God: Comparative Studies in the Religions and Folklore of Dacia and Eastern Europe
sources.description: Provides context for understanding Romanian mythology and the role of female supernatural figures.
sources.text: Provides context for understanding Romanian mythology and the role of female supernatural figures.

sources.author: Romulus Vulcănescu
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of Romanian mythology, including detailed descriptions of various mythical creatures, including the Iele.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of Romanian mythology, including detailed descriptions of various mythical creatures, including the Iele.

sources.author: Marcel Detienne
sources.work: The Masters of Truth in Archaic Greece
sources.description: While focused on Greek mythology, Detienne's work explores themes of liminality and the dangers of encountering supernatural beings, providing a comparative framework for understanding the Iele.
sources.text: While focused on Greek mythology, Detienne's work explores themes of liminality and the dangers of encountering supernatural beings, providing a comparative framework for understanding the Iele.
